All I can see on my side is your manual log of 359 minutes, right around 6 h, with no heart rate, recovery, or baseline data to add nuance, so your own body is the better instrument today. A single shortish night usually doesn't wreck a long aerobic ride; it mostly dulls the top end. If you're still heavy-legged and foggy after thirty minutes, cut the route down rather than grinding through. Either way, prioritize an earlier night tonight.

One rough night doesn't mean anything is wrong — sleep gets a bit lighter and more interruptible with age, and a single scrappy night is something every sleeper has. Going by your log you still recorded about 402 minutes, which is a reasonable night on paper even if it felt broken; since it's manually logged I'd treat that as an estimate rather than a precise read. The most useful habit here isn't chasing last night, it's anchoring the next ones: keep your wake time fixed and get outside light in the morning, which steadies the body clock more than any bedtime rule. Your sister's worry applies to a different picture — months of unrefreshing sleep, loud snoring or gasping, or heavy daytime sleepiness are worth raising at a check-up. One bad night after which you're functioning is just a bad night.
